note: 2.1-3.1: stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that contain "fall" + "into a quandary" (a's: 2.1, 3) and a single one word line, "they were confounded" (b: 3.1)

4-5.1: compound stanza in abb, 1 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets substantially repeated whose lines alternatingly contain one of two names (b's: 4.1-4.2, 5-5.1) and a single line that contains both names (a: 4).

5.2-6.1: compound stanza in abba, 1 + 2 + 2 + 1, by contrast between single lines that begin with "That day" (a's: 5.2, 6.1) and couplets that cohere respectively by enjambment and repetition (b's: 5.3-5.4, 5.5-6)

7-7.3: stanza in abbb by contrast between lines that begin with 3rd person singular verbs in the habitual aspect (b's: 7.1, 7.2, 7.3) and a single line that begins with a second person singular verb in the present continuative.
8-8.2: stanza in aba by parallel lines (a's: 8, 8.2) with opposing verbs "she set out" and "she returned" contrasted with a single verb initial line (b: 8.1).
12.3-13.4: stanza in abbaab, 1 + 2 + 2 + 1, by contrast between lines that contain a 3rd person plural verb (a's: 12.3, 13.2, 13.3) and lines that are composed of praisenames (b's: 13, 13.1, 13.4).

13.5-14.3: compound stanza in abb, 2 + 2 + 2, by contrast between a single couplet that coheres by parallelism (a: 13.5-13.6) and couplets that each cohere internally by enjambment and are both interrogatives composed as subordinate clause introduced by adverbial marker Ka/ question introduced by emphatic marker Nka (b's: 14-14.1, 14.2-14.3).

20-23.2: compound stanza in aab, 4 + 3 + 1, by initial linegroups (a's: 20-21.1, 22-23.1) that cohere internally by enjambment and are composed similarly as scene-setting address or aside (20-21.1, 22)/ parallel quoted speech introduced by locutive (21, 23)/ final parallel line (21.1, 23.1), contrasted with a single finalizing line (b: 23.2) that has an initial negative verb.
25-25.3: stanza in aabb formed by contrast between couplets that cohere as 3rd person singular narrative (a's: 25-25.2) and as negative, quoted, 1st person speech (b's: 25.2-25.3).

26.1-30.1: stanza in aaabbxaaab formed by lines that contain ethical statements (a's: 26.1-26.3, 29-30) contrasted with lines that are composed as "they gave" + fishname [/ praisenames] (b's: 27-28, 30.1); note intervening line (x: 28).
32-32.3: stanza in aaab formed by contrast between lines whose initial 3rd person verb is near past (a's: 32-32.2) and a single line whose initial 3rd person verb is distant past

35.1-37.5: verse in aba formed by contrast between stanzas formed in aba (a's: 35.1-36.2, 37.2-37.5) and a couplet (b: 37-37.1) that coheres by parallel use of 3rd plural near past verbs; the first stanza is formed in aaaba by contrast between 1st person singular verb initial lines that express desire (a's: 35.1-36, 36.2) and a single line that comprises a dependent clause (b: 36.1); the second stanza is formed in aaba by contrast between lines composed as verb initial + placenames (a's: 37.2, 37.3, 37.5) and a single line composed as placenames alone (b: 37.4).

41-46.1: stanza in abbbaabbabbb formed by contrast between lines composed as [child] + "surprised by" + nature spirit (a's: 41, 42.1, 43, 45) and lines composed as praisenames (b's: 41.1, 41.2, 42, 44, 44.1, 45.1, 46, 46.1).

47-48.1: compound stanza in abb, 1 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between nearly identical couplets that cohere internally semantically (b's: 47.1-47.2, 48-48.1) and a single line that begins with a direct object.
48.2-48.4: stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that have an initial verb (a's: 48.2, 48.3) and a single line that has no verb (b: 48.4).
50-51.1: stanza in abbabbb formed by contrast between lines that comprise and address to the maiden (a's: 50, 50.3) and lines composed as praisenames (b's: 50.1, 50.2, 5.4, 51, 51.1).

50-72: verse passage in abba formed, with many interpolated lines, around the contrast between speakers, Mugasha's utterances (a's) of proposal (50, 50.3) and oath (69.1-70.1) and the maiden's utterances (b's) of response (53.2, 53.3)) and oath( 56-57.2).
52-52.3: stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines whose main verbs are inflected to accord with "child" (a's: 52, 52.1) and one line whose verb accords with "spit" (b: 52.3).

53.2-54.1: compound stanza in abb, 1 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets each composed as second person predication / explication of predication (b's: 53.3-53.4 and 55-55.1:fisherman cannot marry royalty and dried fish stink, respectively) contrasted with a single line of address (a: 53.2)
79-81.3: stanza in aba formed by single lines that include a drum name (a's: 79, 81.3) contrasted with lines of drum sounds (b's: 79.1-81.2)
82-114: passage of lines composed as apperance of nature spirit/ [praisename]/ [quoted self-praise of characterization or boast]/: "you marry her, [Mugasha or his praisename]"
117-121: compound stanza in aba, 3 + 2 + 3, formed by contrast between three lines that repeat fully (a's: 117-118.1, 120-121) and two lines that almost repeat (b's: 119-119.1).

124.1-143.1: verse passage composed of questions (a's) and answers (b's) formed into three distinct patterns: aaaab (124.1-127); ababababab (128-137); and a pair of mirror stanzas formed in aabab (138-140.2 and 141-143.1).

150-161: song (perhaps quoted from a folktale) composed in three repeated verses (150-153, 154-157, 158-161) each formed as abab by contrast between lines that contain nouns (a's: 150, 152, 154, 156, 158, 160) and lines that contain only verbs (b's: 151, 153, 155, 157, 159, 161).

167-186.3: passage composed of four linegroups (167-168.1, 169-175.2, 176-181.2, 182-187.3) each composed thematically as (a) fish fulfills wedding role (167, 169 and 173, 176, 182 and 185 - in the second and the fourth linegroups this element is repeated; (b) description of wedding role (167.1, 170-172.2, 177 183-84) (c) transformantion of form (168, 174-175.1, 178-181.1, 186-186.2); (d) "The power of Wamala" (168.1, 175.2, 181.2, 186.3).

190-212.2: verse passage composed of three linegroups (190-198, 199-203, 204-212.3) that form an aab pattern; each linegroup composed as (a) Mugasha sends a nature spitrit (190-190.1, 199, 204) (b) the spirit's action briefly described (191, 200, 205-206) (c) aside commenting on the effects of such an action (192-193, 201, 207) (d) elaborated description of element's action (194-197, 202-202.2, 207-211) (e) Wamala's response (198, 203, 212-212.4); the linegroups create a pattern of aab by contrast principally between simple refusals and an elaborate quasi-acceptance (e: 198, 203 and 121-212.3, respectively), and also between single lines of terse narrative and a pair of lines of direct discourse that cohere by enjambment (b: 191, 200 and 206-207, respectively).

213-221.1: three stanzas formed in aab by lines that cohere by parallelism created by verb inflection and word order contrasted with single lines composed differently; the first such stanza (213-217) is formed in aaab(b) by contrast between lines that have a subject in initial position followed by an adverbial past perfect verb (a's: 213, 214, 215) and a single line that also begins with a subject but whose verb is inflected differently (b: 216); the stanza is then ®MDUL¯re-finalized by a line that has a verb in initial position (b: 217); the second stanza (218-219.1) is formed in aab by contrast between lines of narration that cohere by initial verbs with similar inflections (a's: 218, 219) contrasted with a single line of quoted speech (b:219.1); the third stanza in the group (220.1-221.1) is formed in aab by contrast between lines of narration that begin with a double verb formation (a's:220.1, 222) and a single line of quoted speech (b: 221.1)

249.1-250.1: compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets composed as repeated line + subject final line (a's: 249.1-249.2, 249.3-249.4) and a single couplet of praisenames (b: 250-251).

250.2-252.3: verse of three stanzas (250.2-250.5, 251-251.3, 252-252.3) in aba formed by two stanzas that cohere by lexical repetition and by stanzaic patterning as abba (a's: 250.2-250.5, 252-252.3; formed internally by contrast between parallel lines of placenames (b's: 250.3, 250.4 and 252.1, 252.2) and initial and final lines that cohere by past verbal tenses (a's: 250.2, 250.5 and 252, 252.3)) contrasted with a single stanza in abab, (b: 251-251.3; formed internally by contrast between lines that cohere by grammatical subordination (a's: 251, 251.2) and lines of praisenames (b's:251.1, 251.3)).
254-257.1: stanza in abbba formed by contrast between lines that end with "my child" (a's: 254, 257.1) and lines that begin with "See..." (b's: 255, 256, 257).

259-270: compound stanza in aaba formed by the contrast between three linegroups, each comprised of a single exchange of quoted utterances asymmetrical in length (a's: 259-261, 262-264, 268-270; in the final linegroup the answer is several lines in length, reversing the pattern of the previous linegroups) and a single linegroup that comprises multiple exchanges of quoted utterances symmetrical in length (b: 264.1-267).

283.1-291.1: passage of four linegroups (283.1-284.1, 285-286.1, 287-288.1, 289-291.1) in aaab formed by linegroups that are each composed, with some variation, of three repeated lines (a's: 283.1-284.1, 285-286.1, 287-288.1) contrasted with a single linegroup (b: 289-291.1) that begins like the others but ends differently.
292-299.1: passage of four linegroups (292-293, 294-295, 296-297, 298-299) each each composed, with some variation, of three types of lines formed by repetition and parallelism.

301-305: compound stanza in aab, 2 + 2 + 1, formed by contrast between couplets composed as plant name + resembles + plant name/ repeated line (a's: 301-302, 303-304) and a single line that begins the same but ends differently b: 305).

The bard begins his story at the first of the two trials that reveal the identity of Mugasha's mother. He omits Mugasha's miraculous and difficult birth and early childhood — events on which he lavished, in his earlier performance, almost 200 lines of poetry sung with about 60 of his breaths. In this version he gives less emphasis to the mythic aspects and more to the poetic power of mimesis and comparison. The bard can begin at this point because 1) most people already know the story, and 2) this genre and especially the Mugasha ballad can have various ways of introducing the obligatory, genre-defining episode — the Call — Mugasha's confrontation and mutual swearing with Wamara's daughter.
Mugasha, the spirit that controls events on the lake, being swallowed by the lake is a laughable ruse.
Nyatende is Mugasha's mother's sister. Nyambubi is his mother, as will become apparent.
Child of my mother and ancestral spirit are terms of address that convey both intimacy and respect. Mugasha's aunt asks her sister's help in a difficult task.
My child! Nyatende asserts untruthfully that Mugasha is hers.
This belief about the unsettled nature of a deceased child's spirit is commonly known.
Mugasha's aunt's gesture, hand raised with palm outward, usually requests a spirit's help in redressing a wrong.
A forgetful debtor avoids looking his creditor in the eye.
Kagolo's praise alludes to his hot-headed and warlike behavior, which spirit mediums embody when they are possessed by him.
Dry words are strong and effective, as opposed to ‘wet’ words, which are weak.
Stiff Elbows probably reflects his demeanor as a possessing spirit; it seems to reinforce in gestural language his extreme and immoderate behavior.

The water oracle (Byantabuzi) is associated with this spirit (its name comes from oku-tabula, “to make water bubble up or forth”). Byantabuzi also echoes the name of the place where Kagolo acquired his characteristic way of talking (l. 136.3).
Drank it, spilled it, built it, burned it, refer to the episodes of myths about Kagolo and his grandfather Wamala.

Lyanyaibungo was gathering straw to place on the floor of a dwelling. Work of this kind comes under the category of “to make things pleasing,” a knowledge especially associated with marriageable young women.
Silent runner praises lightning, which in traditional belief is silent and relatively harmless; thunder is thought to make noise and do damage.
Nshungu is a strong seasonal wind that brings cloud formations resembling the long horns of Ankole cattle.
Habib has softened Mugasha's counter-factual vow to appearing naked from committing maternal incest (Mugasha I: 86).
Drink it at the spearshaft of Ndekezi is a proverbial expression for committing suicide. The phrase refers to a poison ordeal administered by a medium of the spirit Kimuli.
The frog's song and the events it alludes to apparently come from a folktale, but I have not been able to find the one.
The house Habib refers to is the traditional Musonge, large, dome-shaped, bamboo-framed, thatched with grass, and apparently very flammable.
The maiden of the hafted knife is a young girl who accompanies the speech-making, spear-bearing elder. Her cultural butterfat becomes natural slime by the power of Wamala.
Looked over with one eye. To look at a person briefly, with the head turned making one eye prominent communicates a hostile, or at least not a friendly, disposition.
Whoever hits your eye... is a well-known proverb.
Mugasha's childless aunt starts out as though she will succeed in becoming known as his mother, but her dedication is not sufficient and she fails. The women argue.
Squatting or sitting with the knees raised is women's characteristic posture for several gender-related acts: giving birth, having sex, urinating, cooking at the hearth, and doing agricultural labor.
